title_en: War, Jihad, and the Defense of the Community
content_en:
Ibn Taymiyyah's discussion of war and jihad in Al-Siyasah al-Shar'iyyah is shaped directly by the historical circumstances of his time: the Mongol invasions and the continued Crusader presence in the Levant. He writes as a scholar who participated in the defense of his community and who saw the theoretical questions of defensive war answered in immediate, practical terms by the situation on the ground.

The Obligation of Jihad

Ibn Taymiyyah treats jihad in the military sense as a collective obligation (fard kifayah) that becomes an individual obligation (fard ayn) when the Muslim community is under direct attack. The latter condition was, in his view, clearly fulfilled in his era — first by the Mongol invasion of Syria and later by the continued Crusader occupation of parts of the Levant. In such circumstances, every capable Muslim in the affected region is obligated to participate in defense, and the obligation extends to neighboring regions if the local defenders are insufficient.

He addresses the question of fighting Mongol rulers who had nominally converted to Islam but continued to govern by Mongol customary law (yasa) rather than Islamic law. Ibn Taymiyyah's famous legal opinions on this matter — that such rulers were to be treated as apostates or rebels rather than legitimate Muslim authorities — had far-reaching consequences for subsequent Islamic political thought, particularly in the twentieth century.

The Conduct of War

Al-Siyasah al-Shar'iyyah contains an extensive treatment of the Islamic rules governing the conduct of war (ahkam al-jihad). Ibn Taymiyyah draws on the Quran and Sunnah to establish the prohibitions: the prohibition on killing non-combatants (women, children, the elderly, monks, and farmers who are not fighting), the prohibition on mutilation of the dead, the rules governing prisoners of war, and the treatment of conquered populations.

He presents these rules not as constraints imposed from outside Islamic ethics but as expressions of the Islamic ethical vision itself: war is permitted for legitimate purposes (defense of the community, removal of oppression, protection of the vulnerable), and these purposes define its limits. A war that degenerates into indiscriminate killing, looting, and destruction has departed from its legitimate basis and becomes mere aggression.

The Commander's Obligations

A substantial section addresses the religious and administrative obligations of military commanders. The commander must maintain discipline among his troops, ensuring they observe the Islamic rules of warfare and do not commit prohibited acts against civilians or captives. He must be just in the distribution of spoils, following the Quranic formula (8:41) that assigns one-fifth to the public treasury and divides the remainder among the fighters. He must maintain the loyalty of his troops through fair treatment and proper provisioning.

Ibn Taymiyyah is insistent that military commanders are accountable to Islamic law even in the heat of war. The necessities of warfare do not suspend the law — they require its careful application under difficult conditions. A commander who orders or permits atrocities against civilians commits a sin that war does not excuse, and scholars have an obligation to speak against such conduct even when doing so is politically or personally dangerous.
content_id:
Pembahasan Ibn Taymiyyah tentang perang dan jihad dalam Al-Siyasah al-Syariyyah dibentuk secara langsung oleh keadaan historis di masanya: invasi Mongol dan kehadiran Tentara Salib yang berkelanjutan di Levant. Ia menulis sebagai seorang ulama yang berpartisipasi dalam pembelaan komunitasnya dan yang menyaksikan pertanyaan-pertanyaan teoretis tentang perang defensif dijawab dalam istilah-istilah yang segera dan praktis oleh situasi di lapangan.

Kewajiban Jihad

Ibn Taymiyyah memperlakukan jihad dalam arti militer sebagai kewajiban kolektif (fard kifayah) yang menjadi kewajiban individual (fard ayn) ketika komunitas Muslim diserang secara langsung. Kondisi yang disebut terakhir, menurut pandangannya, jelas terpenuhi di eranya: pertama oleh invasi Mongol ke Syam dan kemudian oleh pendudukan Tentara Salib yang berkelanjutan di sebagian wilayah Levant. Dalam keadaan seperti itu, setiap Muslim yang mampu di wilayah yang terkena dampak berkewajiban untuk berpartisipasi dalam pertahanan, dan kewajiban itu meluas ke wilayah-wilayah tetangga jika para pembela lokal tidak mencukupi.

Ia membahas pertanyaan tentang memerangi penguasa-penguasa Mongol yang secara nominal telah masuk Islam tetapi terus memerintah berdasarkan hukum adat Mongol (yasa) daripada hukum Islam. Fatwa-fatwa hukum Ibn Taymiyyah yang terkenal dalam hal ini, bahwa penguasa-penguasa seperti itu harus diperlakukan sebagai murtad atau pemberontak daripada penguasa Muslim yang sah, memiliki konsekuensi luas bagi pemikiran politik Islam berikutnya, khususnya pada abad kedua puluh.

Pelaksanaan Perang

Al-Siyasah al-Syariyyah mengandung pembahasan yang luas tentang aturan-aturan Islam yang mengatur pelaksanaan perang (ahkam al-jihad). Ibn Taymiyyah mengacu pada Al-Quran dan Sunnah untuk menetapkan larangan-larangan: larangan membunuh warga sipil (perempuan, anak-anak, orang tua, para biksu, dan petani yang tidak bertempur), larangan mutilasi terhadap orang yang sudah mati, aturan-aturan yang mengatur tawanan perang, dan perlakuan terhadap populasi yang ditaklukkan.

Ia menyajikan aturan-aturan ini bukan sebagai batasan yang dipaksakan dari luar etika Islam, melainkan sebagai ekspresi dari visi etika Islam itu sendiri: perang diizinkan untuk tujuan-tujuan yang sah (pertahanan komunitas, penghapusan penindasan, perlindungan orang-orang yang rentan), dan tujuan-tujuan ini mendefinisikan batas-batasnya. Perang yang merosot menjadi pembunuhan membabi buta, penjarahan, dan penghancuran telah menyimpang dari dasarnya yang sah dan menjadi sekadar agresi.

Kewajiban Panglima

Bagian yang substansial membahas kewajiban-kewajiban keagamaan dan administratif dari para panglima militer. Panglima harus mempertahankan disiplin di antara pasukannya, memastikan mereka mematuhi aturan-aturan Islam dalam peperangan dan tidak melakukan tindakan-tindakan terlarang terhadap warga sipil atau tawanan. Ia harus adil dalam distribusi rampasan, mengikuti rumus Qurani (8:41) yang mengalokasikan seperlima kepada perbendaharaan publik dan membagi sisanya di antara para pejuang. Ia harus mempertahankan loyalitas pasukannya melalui perlakuan yang adil dan perbekalan yang memadai.

Ibn Taymiyyah menegaskan bahwa para panglima militer bertanggung jawab kepada hukum Islam bahkan di tengah panasnya perang. Keharusan-keharusan peperangan tidak menangguhkan hukum, melainkan menuntut penerapan hukum yang cermat dalam kondisi-kondisi yang sulit. Seorang panglima yang memerintahkan atau mengizinkan kekejaman terhadap warga sipil melakukan dosa yang perang tidak dapat membenarkannya, dan para ulama berkewajiban untuk berbicara menentang perilaku semacam itu bahkan ketika melakukannya berbahaya secara politik atau pribadi.
content_ar: ابن تيمية's discussion of war and jihad in Al-Siyasah al-Shar'iyyah is shaped directly by the historical circumstances of his time: the Mongol invasions and the continued Crusader presence in the Levant. He writes as a scholar who participated in the defense of his الجماعة and who saw the theoretical questions of defensive war answered in immediate, practical terms by the situation on the ground. The الالتزام of Jihad ابن تيمية treats jihad in the military sense as a collective الالتزام ( الفرض kifayah ) that becomes an individual الالتزام ( الفرض ayn ) عندما the مسلم الجماعة is under direct attack. The latter condition was, in his view, clearly fulfilled in his era — أولاً by the Mongol invasion of Syria and later by the continued Crusader occupation of parts of the Levant. In such circumstances, every capable مسلم in the affected region is obligated to participate in defense, and the الالتزام extends to neighboring regions إذا the local defenders are insufficient. He addresses the question of fighting Mongol rulers who had nominally converted to الإسلام but continued to govern by Mongol customary law ( yasa ) rather than Islamic law. ابن تيمية's famous legal opinions on this matter — that such rulers were to be treated as apostates or rebels rather than legitimate مسلم authorities — had far-reaching consequences for subsequent Islamic political thought, particularly in the twentieth century. The السلوك of War Al-Siyasah al-Shar'iyyah contains an extensive treatment of the Islamic rules governing the السلوك of war ( ahkam al-jihad ). ابن تيمية draws on the القرآن الكريم and السنة to establish the prohibitions: the prohibition on killing non-combatants (women, الأولاد, the elderly, monks, and farmers who are not fighting), the prohibition on mutilation of the dead, the rules governing prisoners of war, and the treatment of conquered populations. He presents these rules not as constraints imposed from outside Islamic ethics but as expressions of the Islamic ethical vision itself: war is permitted for legitimate purposes (defense of the الجماعة, removal of oppression, protection of the vulnerable), and these purposes define its limits. A war that degenerates into indiscriminate killing, looting, and destruction has departed from its legitimate basis and becomes mere aggression.
